title: Mind the gap: the evolutionary engagement of the C<sub>4</sub> metabolic cycle in support of net carbon assimilation.
author_string: Stata, Matt, Sage, Tammy L, Sage, Rowan F
abstract: C<sub>4</sub> photosynthesis evolved dozens of times, with a critical step being the engagement of a C<sub>4</sub> metabolic cycle to concentrate CO<sub>2</sub> into a bundle sheath-like compartment. While C<sub>3</sub>-C<sub>4</sub> intermediate species show a progressive increase in the activity of a C<sub>4</sub> metabolic cycle, the integration of the C<sub>4</sub> and C<sub>3</sub> biochemical cycles in enhancing photosynthetic carbon gain occurs in a punctuated manner, at an initial C<sub>4</sub> cycle activity near 60%. Punctuated integration of the C<sub>4</sub> cycle could result from the evolutionary acquisition of traits that coordinate the C<sub>3</sub> and C<sub>4</sub> biochemical cycles (for example, an enzymatic, regulatory or transport function) or from a sudden reduction in the mesophyll C<sub>3</sub> cycle. Alternatively, a punctuated pattern could be an artifact of low numbers of C<sub>3</sub>-C<sub>4</sub> intermediates in the evolutionary space where C<sub>4</sub> cycle engagement occurs, due to incomplete sampling of natural diversity or evolutionary dynamics rendering such intermediates unstable. Understanding how the C<sub>4</sub> cycle becomes integrated with the C<sub>3</sub> cycle could reveal new avenues for engineering the C<sub>4</sub> pathway into C<sub>3</sub> plants. Such efforts would be facilitated by the generation of hybrids, or the discovery of additional intermediates, that span the transition from low to high C<sub>4</sub> cycle engagement.
